stem: Let \( \sigma = \left(1\ \ 2\ \ \cdots\ \ [[p]]\right) \) and \( \tau = \left([[p1]]\ \ \cdots\ \ [[pq]]\right) \) be permutations of \( \{1,\dots,[[n]]\} \) written in cycle notation. Since \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\) move disjoint sets of elements, they commute. Find the order of the product \( \sigma\tau \).
solution: Since \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\) have disjoint support, the order of \(\sigma\tau\) is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[p]],[[q]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This adds the cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This is only the length of \(\sigma\), ignoring \(\tau\).
This is only the length of \(\tau\), ignoring \(\sigma\).
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.

stem: Let \( \sigma = \left(1\ \ 2\ \ \cdots\ \ [[p]]\right) \) and \( \tau = \left([[p1]]\ \ \cdots\ \ [[pq]]\right) \) be permutations of \( \{1,\dots,[[n]]\} \) in cycle notation, with disjoint support. Find \( (\sigma\tau)([[k]]) \), the image of \([[k]]\) under the product \(\sigma\tau\) (apply \(\tau\) first, then \(\sigma\)).
solution: \(\tau\) fixes \([[k]]\) (it only moves \([[p1]],\dots,[[pq]]\)), so \((\sigma\tau)([[k]]) = \sigma([[k]])\). Since \(\sigma\) is the cycle \(1\to2\to\cdots\to[[p]]\to1\), this is \([[k]]+1\) unless \([[k]]=[[p]]\), in which case it wraps to \(1\); either way \((\sigma\tau)([[k]]) = [[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to apply \(\sigma\) at all, leaving \([[k]]\) fixed.
This moves \([[k]]\) backward along the cycle instead of forward.
This reflects \([[k]]\) within the cycle instead of advancing it.
This confuses \([[k]]\)'s image with the first element of \(\tau\)'s cycle.
This is off by one above the correct image.
This is off by one below the correct image.

stem: Let \( \sigma = \left(1\ \ 2\ \ \cdots\ \ [[p]]\right) \) be a permutation of \( \{1,\dots,[[p]]\} \) written in cycle notation. Find \( \sigma^{-1}([[k]]) \), the image of \([[k]]\) under the inverse permutation \( \sigma^{-1} \).
solution: \(\sigma^{-1}\) reverses the cycle, sending each element to its predecessor: \([[k]] \to [[k]]-1\), wrapping to \([[p]]\) if \([[k]]=1\). This gives \( \sigma^{-1}([[k]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies \(\sigma\) forward instead of its inverse.
This forgets to apply the inverse at all, leaving \([[k]]\) fixed.
This reflects \([[k]]\) about the midpoint of the cycle instead of taking its predecessor.
This is a common off-by-one variant of the reflection error above.
This is off by one above the correct image.
This is off by one below the correct image.

stem: Let \( \sigma = \left(1\ \ 2\ \ \cdots\ \ [[n]]\right) \) be an \([[n]]\)-cycle, and let \( \rho = \sigma^{[[a]]} \) and \( \tau = \sigma^{[[b]]} \) (also permutations of \( \{1,\dots,[[n]]\} \)). Find the order of the product \( \rho\tau \).
solution: Since \(\sigma^{[[a]]}\sigma^{[[b]]} = \sigma^{[[a]]+[[b]]}\), and \(\sigma^{m}\) is a shift by \(m \bmod [[n]]\) on \([[n]]\) points, the order of \(\rho\tau\) is \( [[n]]/\gcd([[n]], ([[a]]+[[b]]) \bmod [[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to reduce the exponent sum mod \([[n]]\) before taking the gcd.
This is \(|S_{[[n]]}\text{'s cycle}|\) itself, as if \(\rho\tau\) were always the full \([[n]]\)-cycle.
This gives the size of the subgroup generated by the shift, not the order of the shift permutation.
This mistakes the sum of exponents for the order.
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.
